From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Former trade union of the United Kingdom

National Union of General Workers

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Merged into | [National Union of General and Municipal Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_General_and_Municipal_Workers "National Union of General and Municipal Workers") |
| Founded | 1889 |
| Dissolved | 1924 |
| Headquarters | 28 [Tavistock Square](/wiki/Tavistock_Square "Tavistock Square"), London |
| Location | * [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") |
| Members | 39,805 (1907)[[1]](#cite_note-1) 196,090 (1924) |
| Key people | [Will Thorne](/wiki/Will_Thorne "Will Thorne"), General Secretary |
| Affiliations | [TUC](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress"), [NTWF](/wiki/National_Transport_Workers%27_Federation "National Transport Workers' Federation"), [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") |

The **National Union of General Workers** (NUGW) was an early [general union](/wiki/General_union "General union") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the most important general union of its era.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Union_of_General_Workers_(UK)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The union was founded in 1889 as the **National Union of Gas Workers and General Labourers** by [Will Thorne](/wiki/Will_Thorne "Will Thorne"), [Ben Tillett](/wiki/Ben_Tillett "Ben Tillett") and William Byford, following lay offs at [Beckton](/wiki/Beckton "Beckton") [gas works](/wiki/Gas_works "Gas works"). Thorne was elected as the General Secretary, a post he held throughout the life of the union, and successfully argued that the organisation should campaign for an eight-hour working day, rather than an increase in wages. This demand was quickly won, and membership soon rose to over 20,000.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

While the union organised members across the UK, its main areas of strength were London and Lancashire. In London, Thorne was its best-known figure, followed by [Pete Curran](/wiki/Pete_Curran "Pete Curran"), [Arthur Hayday](/wiki/Arthur_Hayday "Arthur Hayday"), [Jack Jones](/wiki/Jack_Jones_(Silvertown_MP) "Jack Jones (Silvertown MP)") and Harry Picard, while in Lancashire its main figures were [J. R. Clynes](/wiki/J._R._Clynes "J. R. Clynes"), [Charles Dukes](/wiki/Charles_Dukes "Charles Dukes"), [Fleming Eccles](/wiki/Fleming_Eccles "Fleming Eccles") and [Arthur Seabury](/wiki/Arthur_Seabury "Arthur Seabury"). It also had significant numbers of members in the north east, organised by [Hugh Lynas](/wiki/Hugh_Lynas "Hugh Lynas"), and in Scotland, where it was organised by [John McKenzie](/wiki/John_McKenzie_(trade_unionist) "John McKenzie (trade unionist)"). Its largest section of members worked in engineering, followed by gas workers, electricity supply, shipyard workers in the south of England and in Scotland. Other industries in which it had a significant membership included the metal trades in Sheffield and Birmingham, aluminium, asbestos and cement works, brickmaking, quarrying, boxmaking, chemicals, rubber, leather, and food and drink manufacturing.[[3]](#cite_note-nugmw-3)

In 1916, the organisation renamed itself the "National Union of General Workers", merging with the [Amalgamated Union of Machine and General Labourers](/wiki/Amalgamated_Union_of_Machine_and_General_Labourers "Amalgamated Union of Machine and General Labourers"). Further mergers followed, principally with the [British Labour Amalgamation](/wiki/British_Labour_Amalgamation "British Labour Amalgamation"), the [Amalgamated Society of Gas, Municipal and General Workers](/wiki/Amalgamated_Society_of_Gas,_Municipal_and_General_Workers "Amalgamated Society of Gas, Municipal and General Workers"), the [Amalgamated Enginemen, Cranemen, Boilermen, Firemen and Wire Rope Workers Union](/w/index.php?title=Amalgamated_Enginemen,_Cranemen,_Boilermen,_Firemen_and_Wire_Rope_Workers_Union&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amalgamated Enginemen, Cranemen, Boilermen, Firemen and Wire Rope Workers Union (page does not exist)") and the [National Federation of Women Workers](/wiki/National_Federation_of_Women_Workers "National Federation of Women Workers").[[4]](#cite_note-marsh-4) This last merger was particularly significant, as it brought 30,000 women into the union.[[3]](#cite_note-nugmw-3)

Unlike many other unions, the NUGW only had a small staff at its headquarters, consisting of Clynes, Jones, Thorne and [Will Sherwood](/wiki/Will_Sherwood "Will Sherwood"), later joined by [Margaret Bondfield](/wiki/Margaret_Bondfield "Margaret Bondfield") from the National Union of Women Workers.[[3]](#cite_note-nugmw-3)

In 1924, the union joined with the [National Amalgamated Union of Labour](/wiki/National_Amalgamated_Union_of_Labour "National Amalgamated Union of Labour") and the [Municipal Employees Association](/wiki/Municipal_Employees_Association "Municipal Employees Association") to form the [National Union of General and Municipal Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_General_and_Municipal_Workers "National Union of General and Municipal Workers").[[4]](#cite_note-marsh-4) Much of the leadership of the new union came from the NUGW, which adopted its districts. Eccles, [R. H. Farrah](/wiki/R._H._Farrah "R. H. Farrah"), Hayday, [William E. Hopkin](/wiki/William_E._Hopkin "William E. Hopkin"), Tom Hurley, Lynas, McKenzie, Walt Wood and S. J. Wright all continued in post as district secretaries, Thorne continued as general secretary, and Clynes as president, while Bondfield, Jones and Sherwood were appointed as assistant general secretaries.[[3]](#cite_note-nugmw-3)

## Election results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Union_of_General_Workers_(UK)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Election results")]

The union sponsored [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") candidates in numerous Parliamentary elections, several of whom won election.

## Presidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Union_of_General_Workers_(UK)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Presidents")]

:   1889: Mark Hutchins
:   1891: Will Watkinson
:   1894: [Pete Curran](/wiki/Pete_Curran "Pete Curran")
:   1910: [J. E. Smith](/wiki/J._E._Smith "J. E. Smith")
:   1912: [J. R. Clynes](/wiki/J._R._Clynes "J. R. Clynes")
